source: Flocabulary G6 + Wordly Wise Book 6 + Common Core Tier 2

words.definition: to figure out something that is not directly stated by using clues
words.example: From the dark clouds, we can infer that it will rain soon.

words.definition: to reach a conclusion by reasoning from the evidence
words.example: The detective deduced who stole the necklace from the fingerprints.

words.definition: to give more detail or explanation about something
words.example: Can you elaborate on your answer? I want to hear more.

words.definition: to make something necessary or appropriate; to justify
words.example: The situation was serious enough to warrant immediate action.

words.definition: to guess about something without knowing all the facts
words.example: Scientists speculate that there may be water on Mars.

words.definition: to say the opposite of what someone else has said
words.example: The new evidence contradicts the original report.

words.definition: to check if something is true or correct
words.example: Always verify your sources before writing a report.

words.definition: to state something confidently as a fact
words.example: She asserted that the experiment would succeed.

words.definition: having more than one possible meaning; unclear
words.example: The ending of the movie was ambiguous — no one knew what happened.

words.definition: based on facts, not personal feelings or opinions
words.example: A good journalist tries to be objective when reporting the news.

words.definition: the art of using language effectively to persuade people
words.example: The politician used powerful rhetoric to win votes.

words.definition: a strong verbal or written attack against someone's opinion or belief
words.example: The editorial was a polemic against the proposed education cuts.

words.definition: to control or influence someone or something in a clever or unfair way
words.example: The advertisement was designed to manipulate people's emotions.

words.definition: to make something sound bigger, better, or worse than it really is
words.example: He exaggerated the size of the fish he caught.

words.definition: to prove that a statement or argument is wrong
words.example: She refuted his claim with clear scientific evidence.

words.definition: a request for support; the quality of being attractive
words.example: The charity made an appeal for donations after the flood.

words.definition: to gradually weaken or damage something
words.example: Constant negativity can undermine a team's confidence.

words.definition: to accept or admit that something is true
words.example: He acknowledged that he had made a mistake.

words.definition: to give special importance to something
words.example: The teacher emphasized the importance of showing your work.

words.definition: having doubts about whether something is true
words.example: I am skeptical about the claims in that advertisement.

words.definition: a series of rulers from the same family who hold power over time
words.example: The Ming Dynasty ruled China for nearly three hundred years.

words.definition: a form of government ruled by a king or queen
words.example: Britain is a constitutional monarchy with a parliament.

words.definition: a group of countries or regions controlled by one ruler
words.example: The Roman Empire stretched across Europe, Africa, and Asia.

words.definition: a sudden and dramatic change, especially in government
words.example: The American Revolution led to the creation of the United States.

words.definition: to send people to live in and control another land
words.example: European countries colonized many parts of Africa and Asia.

words.definition: to leave one's own country to live permanently in another
words.example: Millions of Europeans emigrated to America in the 1800s.

words.definition: a formal agreement between countries
words.example: The two nations signed a peace treaty to end the war.

words.definition: a violent action against the people in power
words.example: The peasants staged a revolt against the unfair taxes.

words.definition: the state of being successful and having plenty of money
words.example: The city enjoyed a period of great prosperity after the new factory opened.

words.definition: cruel and unfair use of power by a ruler
words.example: The people fought to overthrow the tyranny of the king.

words.definition: an educated guess that can be tested through experiments
words.example: My hypothesis is that plants grow faster in sunlight than in shade.

words.definition: something that can change in an experiment
words.example: The amount of water is the variable we are testing.

words.definition: a statement assumed to be true as a basis for reasoning
words.example: Euclid's postulates form the foundation of geometry.

words.definition: numerical facts collected and used for analysis
words.example: The statistics showed a rise in reading scores across the district.

words.definition: the final result or judgment reached after studying evidence
words.example: Based on the data, our conclusion is that salt slows plant growth.

words.definition: a set of rules for correct behavior or procedures in official situations
words.example: Safety protocol requires wearing goggles in the science lab.

words.definition: a sample used for study or testing
words.example: The scientist examined the rock specimen under a microscope.

words.definition: a substance made of two or more elements combined chemically
words.example: Water is a compound made of hydrogen and oxygen.

words.definition: an event or fact that can be observed
words.example: The northern lights are a beautiful natural phenomenon.

words.definition: exact and accurate in every detail
words.example: Use a ruler to make precise measurements.

words.definition: a story where characters and events represent bigger ideas or morals
words.example: Animal Farm is an allegory about political power.

words.definition: to give hints about what will happen later in a story
words.example: The dark storm clouds foreshadowed the trouble ahead.

words.definition: the main character in a story
words.example: The protagonist of the novel overcomes many challenges.

words.definition: the character who opposes the main character
words.example: The villain is the antagonist who creates problems for the hero.

words.definition: a reference to a well-known person, place, event, or work of art
words.example: The title 'East of Eden' is an allusion to the Bible.

words.definition: descriptive language that creates pictures in the reader's mind
words.example: The author used vivid imagery to describe the sunset over the ocean.

words.definition: a feeling of excitement or anxiety about what will happen next
words.example: The mystery novel was full of suspense until the very last page.

words.definition: the use of humor or exaggeration to criticize something
words.example: The TV show uses satire to make fun of politics.

words.definition: a repeated idea, symbol, or theme in a work of art or literature
words.example: The motif of water appears throughout the poem.

words.definition: the feeling or idea a word suggests beyond its dictionary meaning
words.example: The word 'home' has a warm connotation, while 'house' feels neutral.

words.definition: who a person is, including their beliefs, values, and background
words.example: Learning about your culture is part of discovering your identity.

words.definition: unfair treatment of people based on their race, gender, or other differences
words.example: Laws were passed to fight discrimination in the workplace.

words.definition: an unfair opinion formed without knowing all the facts
words.example: Prejudice against others is based on ignorance, not truth.

words.definition: an oversimplified and often unfair idea about a group of people
words.example: It is wrong to judge someone based on stereotypes.

words.definition: a special right or advantage available to a particular person or group
words.example: Having access to good education is a privilege not everyone has.

words.definition: a situation where people are not treated equally
words.example: There is still inequality between rich and poor in many countries.

words.definition: made up of many different kinds of people or things
words.example: The city has a heterogeneous population from many cultural backgrounds.

words.definition: to become part of a group or culture by adopting its customs
words.example: New immigrants often work hard to assimilate into their new country.

words.definition: traditions, culture, and values passed down from past generations
words.example: The festival celebrates the heritage of Korean culture.

words.definition: deep awareness of another's suffering and a desire to help
words.example: The nurse showed great compassion for her patients.

words.definition: laws that have been officially made by a government
words.example: New legislation was passed to protect endangered animals.

words.definition: a change or addition made to a law or constitution
words.example: The First Amendment protects freedom of speech.

words.definition: the power to reject a decision or law
words.example: The president used the veto to stop the new bill.

words.definition: agreed upon by everyone in a group
words.example: The jury reached a unanimous decision of not guilty.

words.definition: the complex system of rules and officials in a government
words.example: Too much bureaucracy can slow down important decisions.

words.definition: an official penalty for breaking a rule; or official permission
words.example: The country faced sanctions for violating the agreement.

words.definition: an organized effort to achieve a goal, often political
words.example: The candidate ran an energetic campaign across the state.

words.definition: the skill of managing relationships between countries peacefully
words.example: Diplomacy helped the two countries avoid a war.

words.definition: a set of beliefs or principles held by a group or organization
words.example: The political party published its official doctrine before the election.

words.definition: the right of self-government; independence
words.example: The region fought for autonomy from the central government.

words.definition: using resources in a way that does not harm the future
words.example: Sustainability means meeting our needs without hurting the planet.

words.definition: the protection of natural resources and wildlife
words.example: Conservation efforts helped save the bald eagle from extinction.

words.definition: the variety of different living things in an area
words.example: The rainforest has the greatest biodiversity on Earth.

words.definition: to make something dirty or poisonous
words.example: Chemical spills can contaminate rivers and lakes.

words.definition: to use up a resource until little or none is left
words.example: Overfishing has depleted the population of tuna in the ocean.

words.definition: gas or other substance released into the air
words.example: Car emissions contribute to air pollution in cities.

words.definition: to take back or restore something to a useful state
words.example: The city reclaimed the old factory and turned it into a park.

words.definition: no longer existing because all members of that species have died
words.example: Dinosaurs became extinct about 65 million years ago.

words.definition: relating to the relationship between living things and their environment
words.example: Oil spills cause serious ecological damage to ocean life.

words.definition: the preserved remains of a plant or animal from millions of years ago
words.example: Scientists found a fossil of a fish that lived 200 million years ago.

words.definition: the reason or drive behind a person's actions
words.example: Her motivation for studying hard was to get into a good college.

words.definition: to become aware of something through the senses or mind
words.example: Different people perceive the same event in different ways.

words.definition: the power to affect or change a situation
words.example: Her experience in the field gave her leverage in the negotiations.

words.definition: a natural behavior that an animal or person is born with
words.example: Birds build nests by instinct, not by learning.

words.definition: to behave the same way as most other people in a group
words.example: Some teenagers feel pressure to conform to what is popular.

words.definition: something that causes a reaction or response
words.example: A loud noise is a stimulus that makes you turn your head.

words.definition: relating to the ability to notice and understand things through the senses
words.example: Young children develop perceptual skills by exploring their environment.

words.definition: aware of what is happening around you
words.example: She was conscious of the fact that everyone was watching her.

words.definition: to hold back or prevent something from happening
words.example: Fear can inhibit a person from trying new things.

words.definition: to become adjusted to new conditions or surroundings
words.example: It took weeks to acclimate to the higher altitude.

words.definition: the relationship between the size of two things compared to each other
words.example: The proportion of boys to girls in our class is 3 to 2.

words.definition: a comparison of two numbers showing how many times one contains the other
words.example: The ratio of wins to losses this season is 5 to 1.

words.definition: a measurement of size such as length, width, or height
words.example: Measure all three dimensions of the box before packing.

words.definition: equal in value, amount, or meaning
words.example: One dollar is equivalent to one hundred cents.

words.definition: close to the exact number but not perfectly accurate
words.example: The approximate distance from here to the mall is five miles.

words.definition: to create something in a careful and detailed way
words.example: The team formulated a plan to solve the math challenge.

words.definition: to have a close connection or relationship with something else
words.example: Good study habits often correlate with higher test scores.

words.definition: to get something from a source; to figure out using logic
words.example: We can derive the answer from the information given.

words.definition: how likely it is that something will happen
words.example: The probability of flipping heads on a coin is fifty percent.

words.definition: opposite in order or effect
words.example: Addition and subtraction have an inverse relationship.

words.definition: to send out a program on television or radio
words.example: The station will broadcast the game live at seven o'clock.

words.definition: a newspaper article that gives the writer's opinion
words.example: The editorial argued for longer recess at school.

words.definition: made or done by someone whose name is not known
words.example: The generous donation was made by an anonymous person.

words.definition: to communicate by exchanging letters or emails
words.example: The two students correspond with each other every month.

words.definition: to remove parts of a book, movie, or text considered harmful
words.example: Some governments censor the internet to control information.

words.definition: a public disagreement about something many people feel strongly about
words.example: The new school dress code created a lot of controversy.

words.definition: spreading very quickly on the internet
words.example: The funny cat video went viral and was seen by millions.

words.definition: the quality of being trusted and believed
words.example: A news source loses credibility when it reports false information.

words.definition: to change the truth or shape of something so it is misleading
words.example: The photo was distorted to make the building look taller.

words.definition: being open and honest, with nothing hidden
words.example: Transparency in government builds trust with citizens.

words.definition: used to show that the opposite is true
words.example: I'm not angry. On the contrary, I think it's funny.

words.definition: because of; considering something
words.example: In light of the new evidence, the case was reopened.

words.definition: mostly; in general
words.example: For the most part, the students did well on the test.

words.definition: about; concerning a particular topic
words.example: With regard to your question, the answer is yes.

words.definition: not at all; absolutely not
words.example: The project is by no means finished yet.

words.definition: partly; somewhat but not completely
words.example: I agree with you to a certain extent, but not fully.

words.definition: over a long period of time; eventually
words.example: Hard work pays off in the long run.

words.definition: to not appreciate something because you are used to it
words.example: Don't take for granted the clean water you drink every day.

words.definition: to cause something to happen or exist
words.example: The invention of the internet gave rise to social media.

words.definition: to remember or consider something important
words.example: Bear in mind that the deadline is next Friday.

words.definition: in contrast to; rather than
words.example: I prefer facts as opposed to opinions.

words.definition: to be suitable for a particular purpose
words.example: This topic lends itself to a lively class debate.

words.definition: to be caused by; to originate from
words.example: Many health problems stem from a poor diet.

words.definition: because of; as a result of
words.example: Owing to heavy rain, the outdoor event was moved inside.

words.definition: to be essentially about one main thing
words.example: The argument boils down to a question of fairness.

words.definition: in a way that harms or sacrifices something else
words.example: He won the game at the expense of his friendship.

words.definition: to be closely connected and happen together
words.example: Education and opportunity go hand in hand.

words.definition: to start using an idea or plan in real life
words.example: Now it's time to put into practice what we learned.

words.definition: to make it easier for something to happen in the future
words.example: Her research paved the way for new medical treatments.

words.definition: despite what has just been said; nevertheless
words.example: The task was difficult; even so, the team completed it on time.
